stories.headline: Update: Polis clemency signals for Tina Peters draw swift rebuke from state officials
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis is signaling openness to granting clemency to former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters, who is serving a nine-year prison sentence after being convicted of orchestrating a breach of election equipment in an effort to find evidence of fraud in the 2020 presidential election. In a post on X, Polis compared Peters' sentence to that of a former Democratic state lawmaker convicted of one of the same crimes who received only probation, calling for even application of justice.

The governor's comments drew immediate condemnation from Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser, Secretary of State Jena Griswold and the association representing local election officials, all of whom warned that clemency would send the wrong message ahead of midterm elections. Griswold, who is running for attorney general, called Polis' remarks 'shocking and worrisome,' while Weiser said clemency decisions should be based on 'remorse, rehabilitation, and extenuating circumstances — not on political influence.'

Polis told KUSA-TV on Wednesday that Peters would need to demonstrate 'appropriate contrition' and apologize for her actions as a condition of any successful clemency application. Peters has not expressed remorse, continuing to defend her actions as necessary to investigate alleged fraud. President Trump has pressured Colorado to release Peters, threatening 'harsh measures' and cutting off federal funding to the state.

Peters' lawyers said they welcomed the governor's comments and hope they lead to her sentence being reduced to the roughly 17 months she has already served. U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet, also a candidate to succeed the term-limited Polis, said Peters should not be pardoned or have her sentence commuted.

stories.headline: Colorado Democrats unveil ballot measure to redirect TABOR refunds to K-12 education for 10 years
stories.summary:
Colorado Senate Democrats introduced legislation Thursday that would ask voters this fall to redirect billions of dollars in TABOR refunds to K-12 public education for the next decade. Under Senate Bill 26-135, the amount the state could retain beyond the Taxpayer's Bill of Rights spending cap would equal what Colorado spends on K-12 education — approximately $4.7 billion in the current budget year — effectively eliminating TABOR refunds for roughly 10 years and directing an estimated $2 billion to schools.

The bill is sponsored by Sens. Jeff Bridges of Greenwood Village and Cathy Kipp of Fort Collins, with House sponsors Reps. Jennifer Bacon of Denver and Meghan Lukens of Steamboat Springs. Supporters unveiled the proposal before approximately 50 teachers from around the state, with Colorado Education Association President Kevin Vick arguing that Colorado schools are underfunded by as much as $4 billion and that the state ranks near the bottom nationally in teacher pay and retention.

Republican lawmakers immediately opposed the measure. Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer of Brighton, a member of the Joint Budget Committee, called the proposal 'another example of misplaced priorities at the Capitol,' arguing the state already has sufficient funds but lacks political will to prioritize education. She and other conservatives contend the plan sidesteps constitutional spending limits.

Despite facing unified Republican opposition among the legislature's 34 GOP members, the bill is expected to advance given Democrats' overwhelming majorities in both chambers. If passed, it would go before voters in November.

stories.headline: Update: Colorado measles outbreak grows to seven cases at Broomfield schools; exposure sites include DIA
stories.summary:
Colorado public health officials identified four new measles cases Friday linked to an outbreak centered at Broomfield High School and Broomfield Heights Middle School, bringing the total number of infected individuals in the cluster to seven. All newly identified cases involve unvaccinated children between the ages of 5 and 17; three live in Adams County and one in Weld County.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment said the latest cases were identified through contact tracing but that investigators have still not determined the original source of the outbreak. Officials believe it most likely originated from an undetected community member or traveler who had measles in early February. Unvaccinated students, staff, volunteers and visitors have been told to stay away from both schools for 21 days.

CDPHE released an updated list of potential exposure sites that includes medical facilities, teen hangouts, a church and Denver International Airport. Because the exposure windows occurred at least one week ago, new infections could continue to emerge for nearly two more weeks. People who may have been exposed are urged to monitor for symptoms — fever, cough, runny nose, red eyes and rash — and to call CDPHE before seeking medical care.

Colorado has now recorded eight measles cases in 2026, nearly a quarter of the 36 cases seen in all of 2025, which was already the state's highest annual total since the 1990s. Nationally, the CDC has reported 1,281 cases so far in 2026, already more than half of last year's full-year total.

stories.headline: Friday snowstorm brings 3-5 inches to Denver, disrupts commutes and grounds flights at DIA
stories.summary:
A winter storm swept across the Denver metro area Friday, ending a 40-day stretch without measurable snowfall and delivering 3 to 5 inches of heavy, wet snow to the city. The National Weather Service in Boulder issued a Winter Weather Advisory through 8 p.m., with higher totals of 4 to 6 inches possible in the western and southern portions of the metro. Colorado's central mountains received up to 14 inches in some areas.

Denver International Airport issued a ground stop during the height of the storm, and a United Airlines plane struck a deicing truck on the airfield Friday morning, sending one person to the hospital. Several Front Range highways faced partial closures and significant delays: Interstate 25 was closed northbound at the Wyoming border before reopening at midday, and U.S. 36 was shut eastbound between Estes Park and Boulder following a crash.

The afternoon commute saw heavy backups, with I-25 and portions of I-70 reporting significant slowdowns by 4 p.m. Temperatures remained near or just above freezing throughout the day, with a high of 33 degrees — roughly 19 degrees below the historical average for March 6 in Denver. Accumulation fell primarily on grass and vehicles, with road surfaces seeing heavier buildup during the most intense snowfall periods.

Meteorologists said precipitation would taper off by 8 p.m. Friday, with milder and drier conditions expected to return Saturday with a high of 50 degrees. Warming is forecast to continue into early next week, with Denver potentially reaching 71 degrees by Monday.

stories.headline: Denver metro housing market surges in February as mortgage rates fall below 6%
stories.summary:
The Denver metro real estate market posted strong gains in February, with pending sales jumping more than 29 percent from January and more than 15 percent year-over-year, according to the monthly report from the Denver Metro Association of Realtors. A key driver was mortgage rates falling below 6 percent by month's end — the first time in more than a year — which combined with lower median sale prices to draw buyers back into the market.

Median sale prices declined 5 percent for attached homes and 2 percent for detached homes compared to a year ago. Closed sales rose nearly 30 percent month-over-month, and the close-price-to-list-price ratio climbed to 98.7 percent, up from 97.9 percent in January. The number of days homes spent on the market fell sharply — down 30 percent for attached homes and 40 percent for detached homes.

New listings increased 12 percent from January, providing buyers with more options heading into spring. DMAR Market Trends Committee chair Amanda Snitker said well-priced, move-in-ready homes in prime locations were drawing multiple offers, while overpriced or outdated properties lingered. She noted that buyers who entered the market early in 2026 benefited most from softer pricing and lower rates.

Despite the February surge, year-to-date figures for 2026 remain below 2025 levels, with sales volume down about 10 percent and the median close price down about 3 percent. The luxury segment showed notable strength, with closings of homes priced between $1.5 million and $1.9 million rising nearly 66 percent in February.

stories.headline: Update: Aaron Gordon returns to Nuggets lineup against Knicks after 17-game absence
stories.summary:
Aaron Gordon was cleared to play Friday night against the New York Knicks, ending a 17-game absence due to a right hamstring strain and marking his first game action since early February. Gordon was not formally cleared until approximately one hour before tipoff, and coach David Adelman said he would play on an unspecified minutes restriction. With Cam Johnson also returning from a minor ankle injury, Friday marked the first time Denver's opening-night starting lineup was available since Nov. 12 — ending a streak of 52 consecutive games without a full starting unit.

Gordon's importance to the team is reflected clearly in the numbers: Denver is 17-6 when he plays and 22-18 when he does not. The Nuggets' offensive rating is 3.9 points per 100 possessions better with him on the court, and their defensive rating improves by 8.2 points. Nikola Jokic said the team missed Gordon's length and defensive versatility most, alongside the injured Peyton Watson.

Gordon missed his 40th game of the season Thursday and his 71st over the past two seasons before returning Friday. Despite his injury struggles, teammates said his presence on the bench remained a constant source of guidance. Guard Julian Strawther, who started in Gordon's place, said Gordon had been 'in everybody's ear' during his absence.

'Aaron's Aaron. He's a physical presence. He's shooting the ball really well,' said Christian Braun. 'He kind of holds our offense together in a way because he plays in the dunker and can catch lobs and adds a new dynamic.' The Nuggets entered Friday's back-to-back against New York having beaten the Lakers 120-113 the night before.

stories.headline: Broncos free agency preview: Denver targets interior linebacker, running back as legal tampering window nears
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos enter the 2026 NFL free agency period with approximately $28 million in cap space and what team sources describe as a 'significant appetite' for an interior linebacker, according to the Denver Post. The legal tampering window opens at 10 a.m. MT Monday, with new contracts eligible to be signed starting Wednesday at 2 p.m. Owner Greg Penner has framed the team's approach as 'opportunistically aggressive.'

At running back, the Broncos face the likely departure of J.K. Dobbins and are seeking a true RB1 or complement to second-year back RJ Harvey. Kenneth Walker III of the Seattle Seahawks and Travis Etienne are among the top targets. At quarterback, the team's direction depends partly on whether they trade backup Jarrett Stidham — potentially freeing $6.5 million in cap space — before deciding how to fill out the depth chart behind Bo Nix.

The team is also expected to address tight end and potentially wide receiver in what club insiders view as a two-season window to compete before Nix's contract extension puts significant strain on the long-term cap structure. Multiple NFL teams have already been inquiring about Stidham's availability, adding a layer of uncertainty to Denver's offseason planning.

On the defensive side, the Broncos recently placed a second-round tender on cornerback Ja'Quan McMillian while declining to tender running back Jaleel McLaughlin, signaling their roster priorities. The combination of cap flexibility, a young franchise quarterback and specific positional needs makes this one of the more closely watched free agency periods in Denver in recent years.

stories.headline: Kyle Karros homers twice in spring as Rockies defeat Athletics 11-6
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies returned to the win column in Cactus League play Friday, defeating the Oakland Athletics 11-6 behind four home runs. Prospect Kyle Karros went 2-for-3 with a two-run homer in the fifth inning — his second of the spring — further solidifying his case for the everyday third base job heading into the regular season.

Starter Chase Dollander pitched three innings, allowing one run on four hits with two walks and two strikeouts, giving him a 2.57 ERA for the spring. First base hopeful TJ Rumfield went 1-for-2 with his fourth home run of the spring, drawing two walks and scoring three runs, putting him in the driver's seat for the open first base position.

The Rockies, coming off the worst season in franchise history, are using the spring to evaluate several competition spots on the roster. Colorado returns to action Saturday evening against the Los Angeles Dodgers at 6:05 p.m. MT, with the game airing on MLB Network.

stories.headline: Aurora police double reward to $4,000 in 2024 cold case shooting outside East Colfax convenience store
stories.summary:
The Aurora Police Department doubled its reward to $4,000 Friday for information leading to an arrest in the June 2024 fatal shooting of 27-year-old Preston Guillory outside a convenience store in the 12000 block of East Colfax Avenue. Guillory was shot multiple times around 3:30 a.m. on June 21, 2024, and was pronounced dead at the scene.

Investigators believe several bystanders witnessed the shooting but have not come forward. Surveillance footage captured a potential suspect described as a man wearing a white hooded sweatshirt and black pants who appeared to be hunched over while walking near the incident location. Violent Crimes Section Lt. Sean Mitchell urged witnesses to contact police, saying even small details could be critical to the investigation.

The increased reward is valid for six months. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at (720) 913-7867 and may remain anonymous.

stories.headline: Sundance Film Festival eyes return to pre-pandemic size with 2027 Boulder debut
stories.summary:
The Sundance Film Festival is planning to return to its pre-pandemic scale when it makes its debut in Boulder in 2027, following the announcement last year that the festival would relocate from Park City, Utah, to Colorado. Festival organizers are working with Boulder officials on logistics for a gathering that would be significantly larger than recent editions, according to the Denver Business Journal.

Gov. Jared Polis was among the key figures who announced Boulder as the new host city at a press conference at the Boulder Theater in March 2025. The move is expected to have a substantial economic impact on Boulder and the broader Denver metro region, drawing filmmakers, industry executives and cinephiles from around the world each January.

Details on venue arrangements, ticketing and the full scope of the festival's Boulder footprint are still being finalized ahead of the 2027 event.

stories.headline: Colorado company challenges SpaceX spectrum deal before FCC
stories.summary:
A Colorado-based company has filed arguments with the Federal Communications Commission opposing a billion-dollar spectrum deal involving SpaceX, contending that SpaceX should not be permitted to effectively displace it from spectrum the company currently occupies. The Colorado firm argues that SpaceX's acquisition would amount to being 'essentially evicted' from its established spectrum position.

The dispute involves high-stakes radio frequency allocations with implications for satellite and wireless communications services. SpaceX has been aggressively pursuing spectrum rights to support its expanding Starlink broadband satellite network, which has made it a central figure in multiple FCC proceedings.

The outcome of the FCC proceeding could have significant consequences for Colorado-based telecommunications and satellite companies competing in an increasingly crowded spectrum environment.

stories.headline: Denver Restaurant Week opens Friday with more than 270 participating metro eateries
stories.summary:
Denver Restaurant Week kicked off Friday, with more than 270 restaurants across the metro area offering specially priced multi-course menus for the duration of the annual event. The promotion, one of the city's most popular culinary traditions, gives diners across the Front Range an opportunity to explore a wide range of cuisines at set price points.

Restaurants from downtown Denver to suburban neighborhoods throughout the metro are participating, spanning a broad spectrum of cuisine types and price tiers. The event typically draws tens of thousands of diners and provides a significant revenue boost for participating establishments, many of which see it as an opportunity to attract new repeat customers.

Reservations for popular restaurants have historically filled up quickly during Restaurant Week. Diners are encouraged to book in advance through restaurant websites or reservation platforms.

weather.notable: Nearly 6 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I don't trust stairs. They're always up to something.

topStory.headline: Talarico Defeats Crockett in Texas Democratic Senate Primary
topStory.summary:
James Talarico, a former middle school teacher and current Texas state House member, defeated U.S. Rep. Jasmine Crockett and Ahmad Hassan in the Texas Democratic primary for U.S. Senate on March 3, 2026. Talarico's victory came in a closely watched race in which he and Crockett had led the field in polling, fundraising, and media attention heading into primary day.

The two leading candidates represented notably different strategic visions for how Democrats should contest statewide office in Texas. Crockett, first elected to Congress in 2022, built a national profile as a combative critic of the Trump administration and argued her progressive, high-energy approach would drive Democratic turnout in major urban centers. Talarico pursued a broader coalition strategy, seeking to energize the Democratic base while also courting disaffected Republican-leaning voters.

Polling analysis offered a counterintuitive picture of the ideological dynamics at play. Writing on primary day, New York Times analyst Nate Cohn noted that Talarico performed best among self-identified liberals while Crockett drew stronger support from moderate and conservative Democrats, suggesting the race did not divide cleanly along ideological lines despite the candidates' contrasting styles.

Talarico now advances to the general election against Republican incumbent John Cornyn, who is seeking another term. The race faces long odds for Democrats: The Cook Political Report, Inside Elections, and Larry Sabato's Crystal Ball each rated the contest Likely Republican as of March 2026. Recent history reinforces that assessment, with Republicans winning the last two Texas Senate contests by margins of roughly nine to ten percentage points.

The outcome carries significance beyond Texas, with Politico describing Talarico's general election campaign as one of the sharpest national tests of whether a Democratic candidate in a red state can run with some distance from the national party's brand. Talarico's campaign has centered on a message of fixing broken institutions and returning power to communities, framing that his team believes can appeal across traditional partisan lines.
